_meta.description: Architectural and structural design patterns for construction-scale 3D printing. Agent-consumable patterns for designing printed buildings and structures.

load_bearing_wall.description: A structural wall that carries roof/floor loads directly to the foundation. The primary structural element in most printed buildings.
agent_guidance: Double-bead (200mm) is the standard for single-story residential. Two parallel beads with air gap or core fill.
Every wall needs a bond beam at the top — continuous reinforced concrete beam that ties wall to roof structure.
Openings (doors, windows) need lintels/headers. Print up to the opening, place steel header, continue printing above.
Height-to-thickness ratio of 18:1 max. A 200mm wall can go 3.6m tall. Thicker walls go taller.
Corner connections: overlap alternating layers (like brick bonding) to create structural interlocking.
Vertical rebar in cells every 600mm for walls over 2.5m. Grouted after printing.

curved_wall.description: A non-rectilinear wall following a curved path. One of the major advantages of 3D printing over traditional construction — curves cost the same as straight walls.
agent_guidance: THIS IS WHERE PRINTED CONSTRUCTION SHINES. Curves cost zero extra vs straight walls. In traditional construction, curves cost 3-5x more.
Use curves to your advantage — curved walls are inherently stronger than flat walls against lateral loads (wind, seismic).
Minimum practical radius is 0.5m for most printers. Below that, the nozzle can't maintain consistent bead width.
Slow down 20% in tight curves to maintain layer quality. The outside of the curve gets stretched, inside gets compressed.
Double-curved surfaces (domes, shells) require specialized toolpath planning — most slicers don't handle this natively.
Acoustic benefits: curved interior walls eliminate parallel surfaces that cause flutter echo. Design for sound quality.
Freeform organic shapes are the architectural signature of printed construction. Lean into it for visual differentiation.

window_opening.description: An opening in a printed wall for windows or doors. Requires special handling because the printing process can't bridge gaps natively.
agent_guidance: Print walls up to sill height, leave opening, continue walls on each side up to header height.
Place steel or precast header across the opening before continuing to print above.
Standard rough opening: window size + 15mm each side for shimming and sealing.
Max opening width without special engineering: 2.4m. Wider spans need engineered beams.
Arched openings are naturally supported during printing — the arch shape holds the fresh concrete.
Consider printing a temporary support shelf at the opening bottom that gets removed after cure.

insulated_wall_system.description: A wall system combining printed concrete with insulation to meet thermal performance requirements. Multiple strategies exist.

insulation_strategies.description: Spray foam between inner and outer printed shells
Rigid foam board on exterior face with cladding
Rigid foam board on interior face with drywall

agent_guidance: Bare printed concrete walls have R-0.08 per inch. A 200mm wall is about R-0.6. That fails every energy code.
INSULATION IS MANDATORY for habitable buildings. The wall alone does NOT meet thermal requirements.
Best approach for most climates: double-bead wall with spray foam core fill. Gets R-19+ in a 300mm total wall.
Cold climates (zones 6-8): add exterior rigid foam over the printed wall. R-25+ with continuous insulation.
Concrete's thermal mass is an advantage in hot-arid climates — absorbs heat by day, releases at night.
Prevent thermal bridges: insulation must be continuous. Don't leave uninsulated concrete connections between inside and outside.
Vapor barrier goes on the warm side of the insulation. In heating climates: interior. In cooling climates: exterior.

foundation_connection.description: The connection between the printed walls and the foundation. Printers print walls, not foundations — the foundation is always built traditionally first.
agent_guidance: Foundation is ALWAYS traditional construction. Printed structures sit on top of a conventional foundation.
Level the foundation to within 5mm across the full building footprint. Printers can't compensate for an unlevel base.
Set anchor bolts or J-bolts in the foundation at 600mm spacing to tie the first printed layer down.
Rebar dowels extend up from foundation into the wall cells — grouted after printing for full structural connection.
Moisture barrier between foundation and first printed layer prevents rising damp.
Slab-on-grade is most common for residential. Stem wall for raised floor. Pier-and-beam for difficult sites.

roof_connection.description: How the roof structure connects to the top of printed walls. A critical structural detail that transfers wind and gravity loads.
agent_guidance: Bond beam at top of wall is MANDATORY. Poured in place after wall printing completes. Ties everything together.
Embed anchor bolts in bond beam for wood top plate — standard wood framing roof sits on top.
For hurricane/high-wind zones: hurricane straps from truss to bond beam, not just gravity connection.
Flat concrete roofs can be poured on formwork supported by the printed walls — but check wall capacity for wet concrete load.
Steel truss roofs bolt directly to embed plates cast into the bond beam.
The roof-to-wall connection is the most critical structural detail. Failures here cause catastrophic collapse in storms.

utility_chase.description: Channels and voids within or alongside printed walls for routing electrical, plumbing, and HVAC systems.
agent_guidance: Plan utility routing BEFORE printing. Chases must be designed into the wall geometry, not cut after.
Electrical: embed conduit during printing. Small enough to route within the wall beads or between double-bead cavity.
Plumbing: route in the wall cavity or on the interior face. Do NOT embed water pipes in printed concrete — repairs require wall demolition.
HVAC: plan duct routes through the wall cavity for small ducts, or build dedicated mechanical chases for larger systems.
Always maintain 75mm minimum concrete thickness after chase. Thinner walls lose structural capacity.
Access panels at code-required locations for inspection and repair. Design these into the wall during printing.

multi_story_connection.description: Structural connection between floor levels in multi-story printed buildings. The printed walls support floor slabs which then support the next level of walls.
agent_guidance: Print first-story walls. Place floor system. Print second-story walls on top of floor. Repeat.
Precast hollow-core planks are fastest — crane-placed on printed walls, grouted, ready for next level same day.
Rebar must continue vertically through the floor slab into the upper wall. This creates a continuous load path.
3 stories max for pure concrete bearing walls. Above that, need a hybrid steel frame + printed infill strategy.
Lateral resistance (wind/seismic) requires shear walls, bracing, or moment frames. Pure printed walls are not enough above 2 stories.
Each floor acts as a diaphragm — must be rigidly connected to all walls to distribute lateral forces evenly.
Tolerance stack-up matters: 5mm per floor adds up. Re-survey and shim between levels as needed.
